content:
# Deuteronomy 8 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 8 (20 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA

---

## V.1 — "give to" vs. "give"

- **EMET:** "the land that the LORD swore to your fathers"
- **LOGOS:** "the land that the LORD swore to give to your fathers"
- **PNEUMA:** "the land the LORD swore to give your ancestors"
- **Ruling:** "the land that the LORD swore to give to your fathers" — LOGOS preserves the infinitive latet (to give) which EMET omits. PNEUMA's "ancestors" for avot is set aside in favor of "fathers" per project convention.

---

## V.2 — "test you to know" vs. "probing what was in your heart"

- **EMET:** "to humble you, to test you, to know what was in your heart"
- **LOGOS:** "that he might humble you, testing you to know what was in your heart"
- **PNEUMA:** "humbling you, testing you, probing what was in your heart"
- **Ruling:** "to humble you, to test you, to know what was in your heart" — EMET preserves the three parallel infinitives (le'annothkha, lenasothkha, lada'at). PNEUMA's "probing" is interpretive. LOGOS compresses two infinitives into a participial clause.

---

## V.3 — "let you be hungry" vs. "let you hunger"

- **EMET:** "let you be hungry"
- **LOGOS:** "let you hunger"
- **PNEUMA:** "by letting you go hungry"
- **Ruling:** "let you hunger" — "hunger" as a verb is slightly more natural English. Marginal difference.

---

## V.3 — "going-forth of the mouth" vs. "word that comes from the mouth"

- **EMET:** "but by every going-forth of the mouth of the LORD a man lives"
- **LOGOS:** "but man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** "but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD"
- **Ruling:** "but by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD" — EMET's "going-forth" (motsa') is rigidly literal. LOGOS and PNEUMA render it as "word that comes" — both accurate and more natural. LOGOS adopted.

---

## V.4 — "clothing" vs. "garment"

- **EMET:** "Your garment did not wear out"
- **LOGOS:** "Your clothing did not wear out"
- **PNEUMA:** "The clothing on your back did not wear out"
- **Ruling:** "Your clothing did not wear out" — "clothing" is more natural English for simlathkha. PNEUMA's "on your back" adds specificity not in the Hebrew.

---

## V.5 — opening conjunction

- **EMET:** "And you shall know in your heart"
- **LOGOS:** "Know then in your heart"
- **PNEUMA:** "Know in your heart"
- **Ruling:** "Know then in your heart" — LOGOS's rendering is best; "and" (EMET) is unnecessary here, and "then" appropriately marks the consequential relationship.

---

## V.6 — "to walk in his ways and to fear him" vs. "by walking in his ways and fearing him"

- **EMET:** "to walk in his ways and to fear him"
- **LOGOS:** "by walking in his ways and by fearing him"
- **PNEUMA:** "walk in his ways and hold him in reverent awe"
- **Ruling:** "by walking in his ways and fearing him" — LOGOS's instrumental "by" correctly captures the Hebrew. PNEUMA's "reverent awe" for yir'ah is fine but slightly longer than needed.

---

## V.7 — "wadis" vs. "brooks" vs. "streams"

- **EMET:** "a land of wadis of water"
- **LOGOS:** "a land of brooks of water"
- **PNEUMA:** "a land with flowing streams"
- **Ruling:** "a land of streams of water" — "streams" is more universally understood than "wadis" (too technical) or "brooks" (too English/mild). "Of water" retained as it is in the Hebrew.

---

## V.7 — "springs and depths" vs. "fountains and springs" vs. "springs and deep waters"

- **EMET:** "of springs and depths going out in the valley and in the hill-country"
- **LOGOS:** "of fountains and springs, flowing out in the valleys and the hills"
- **PNEUMA:** "springs and deep waters welling up in the valleys and hills"
- **Ruling:** "springs and deep waters welling out in the valleys and the hills" — Hebrew ayonot (springs) and tehomot (deep waters/the deep). EMET's "depths" is too vague; LOGOS's "fountains and springs" repeats the same concept; PNEUMA's "welling up" is good.

---

## V.8 — "olive-oil" vs. "olive oil" vs. "olive trees and honey"

- **EMET:** "a land of olive-oil and honey"
- **LOGOS:** "a land of olive oil and honey"
- **PNEUMA:** "a land of olive trees and honey"
- **Ruling:** "a land of olive oil and honey" — Hebrew shemen (oil) not etz shemen (olive tree). PNEUMA's "olive trees" is incorrect; the Hebrew specifies the product.

---

## V.9 — "scarceness" vs. "scarcity"

- **EMET:** "bread without scarceness"
- **LOGOS:** "bread without scarcity"
- **PNEUMA:** "food without scarcity"
- **Ruling:** "bread without scarcity" — "scarcity" is standard modern English; "scarceness" is archaic. PNEUMA's "food" for lechem is an over-expansion; "bread" retained.

---

## V.9 — "hew" vs. "dig" copper

- **EMET:** "from whose hills you may hew copper"
- **LOGOS:** "out of whose hills you can dig copper"
- **PNEUMA:** "from whose hills you can dig copper"
- **Ruling:** "out of whose hills you can dig copper" — "dig" is the majority reading and more natural. Hebrew tachtsov = quarry/dig/hew; both are acceptable but "dig" wins by majority.

---

## V.10 — "be satisfied" vs. "be full"

- **EMET:** "you shall eat and be satisfied"
- **LOGOS:** "you shall eat and be full"
- **PNEUMA:** "When you have eaten your fill"
- **Ruling:** "you shall eat and be satisfied" — Hebrew savata = satisfied/sated. "Satisfied" is more precise than "full."

---

## V.12 — "lest, when you have eaten" vs. "lest you eat"

- **EMET:** "lest you eat and be satisfied"
- **LOGOS:** "lest, when you have eaten and are full"
- **PNEUMA:** "When you have eaten your fill"
- **Ruling:** "lest, when you have eaten and are satisfied" — the lest-construction (pen) must be preserved. PNEUMA drops it.

---

## V.13 — "cattle and flocks" vs. "herds and flocks"

- **EMET:** "your cattle and your flocks multiply"
- **LOGOS:** "when your herds and flocks multiply"
- **PNEUMA:** "when your herds and flocks have multiplied"
- **Ruling:** "when your herds and flocks multiply" — Hebrew baqar (cattle/herd) and tson (flock). "Herds and flocks" is standard and accurate.

---

## V.14 — "heart grows high" vs. "heart be lifted up" vs. "heart will become proud"

- **EMET:** "your heart grows high and you forget"
- **LOGOS:** "then your heart be lifted up, and you forget"
- **PNEUMA:** "your heart will become proud and you will forget"
- **Ruling:** "your heart becomes proud and you forget" — PNEUMA's "proud" is the clearest English rendering of verom levavkhem (your heart is lifted/elevated). LOGOS's "lifted up" is more literal but less idiomatic.

---

## V.15 — "great and fearful" vs. "great and terrifying" vs. "vast and dreadful"

- **EMET:** "the great and fearful wilderness"
- **LOGOS:** "the great and terrifying wilderness"
- **PNEUMA:** "that vast and dreadful wilderness"
- **Ruling:** "the great and fearful wilderness" — Hebrew gadol venora: gadol = great, nora = fearful/awesome/terrifying. "Fearful" is the most established English rendering of nora in this context.

---

## V.15 — "flinty rock" vs. "flint rock" vs. "hard flint rock"

- **EMET:** "from the flint rock"
- **LOGOS:** "out of the flinty rock"
- **PNEUMA:** "out of hard flint rock"
- **Ruling:** "out of the flinty rock" — "flinty" is the established English adjective. PNEUMA's "hard" is redundant; flint is by definition hard.

---

## V.17 — "has made for me" vs. "have gotten me" vs. "produced all this wealth for me"

- **EMET:** "has made for me this wealth"
- **LOGOS:** "have gotten me this wealth"
- **PNEUMA:** "produced all this wealth for me"
- **Ruling:** "have produced for me this wealth" — "produced" (PNEUMA) captures la'asot (to make/do/produce) well. LOGOS's "gotten" is too commercial. EMET's "made" is possible but slightly mechanical. Adopting PNEUMA's verb with word order adjusted.

---

## V.19 — warning formula

- **EMET:** "I testify against you today that you shall surely perish"
- **LOGOS:** "I solemnly warn you today that you shall surely perish"
- **PNEUMA:** "I warn you solemnly today that you will certainly perish"
- **Ruling:** "I solemnly warn you today that you shall surely perish" — LOGOS's "solemnly warn" captures the Hebrew ha'idothi bakem (I testify/warn against you) well. EMET's "testify against" is also correct (legal language) but LOGOS's rendering is more English-natural. "Shall surely perish" preferred to "will certainly perish" (modal consistency).

---

## V.20 — "hearken to" vs. "obey" and "you shall perish" vs. "so you too shall perish"

- **EMET:** "because you did not hearken to the voice of the LORD"
- **LOGOS:** "because you would not obey the voice of the LORD"
- **PNEUMA:** "because you would not obey the voice of the LORD your God"
- **Ruling:** "because you would not obey the voice of the LORD your God" — "obey the voice" is idiomatic English for shema' beqol. PNEUMA adds "your God" which is in the Hebrew (YHWH Eloheikhem). Adopted.

---

**Total disagreements logged: 21**

— SOPHIA
